sections.body: Clustering-based diarization methods assign each frame to exactly one speaker cluster (hard clustering such as AHC or k-means), treating diarization as a set partitioning problem. Consequently, they fundamentally cannot deal with overlapping speech where multiple speakers are active simultaneously.

End-to-end neural diarization (EEND) methods treat diarization as multi-label classification and can handle overlapping speech. However, they perform poorly when the number of speakers is large because: (1) training datasets rarely contain mixtures with many speakers, causing overtraining on few-speaker mixtures; (2) permutation invariant training becomes computationally expensive for many speakers. Extensions like encoder-decoder attractor (EDA) [8] and speaker-conditioned EEND (SC-EEND) [9] partly address flexible speaker counts but still degrade on large speaker sets.

We propose to combine clustering-based and end-to-end diarization methods. First, obtain initial diarization results $\{T_k\subseteq[T]\}_{k=1}^K$ via x-vector clustering (which does not produce overlapping results). Then iteratively select two speakers, pick up frames containing only those two, and process them with a two-speaker EEND model to refine overlapped regions. We assume at most $\tilde{K}<K$ speakers speak simultaneously and set $\tilde{K}=2$.

The EEND model used is SA-EEND-EDA [8]: a 4-layer Transformer encoder extracts frame embeddings, then an encoder-decoder attractor (EDA) module computes attractors. Embedding order is shuffled before the EDA, making all components order-independent, so the model handles non-contiguous selected frames.

The original EEND methods [6,8,9] used matched datasets for adaptation (e.g., only two-speaker subsets). Frame-selection training makes full use of all available data, improving performance without mixture-wise selection.

On the CALLHOME-2spk test set, training with the full CALLHOME dataset using frame selection improved SA-EEND DER from 9.54% to 9.00% and SA-EEND-EDA DER from 8.07% to 7.84%, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed training strategy.

x-vector AHC + VB + Proposed achieved 14.06% DER, outperforming SA-EEND-EDA's 15.29% by 1.23% absolute. Clustering-based methods outperform EEND when the number of speakers exceeds four, but the proposed method performs well on both large and small speaker counts. Improvements were consistent across all speaker counts (2--6 speakers). All results include overlapped regions and are not based on oracle SAD.

On the AMI eval set (headset mix), the proposed method reduced DER by 3.07% (x-vector AHC baseline), 3.14% (AHC+VB), and 0.18% (AHC+VB+overlap detection). Surprisingly, it improved results even when overlap detection [29] was already applied. JER improvements were similar in magnitude.

On DIHARD II eval (10 diverse domains), the proposed method reduced the official baseline DER by 2.96% and JER by 2.91%. It also improved the winning BUT system: DER by 0.35% (w/o OVL) and 0.38% (w/ OVL), JER by 0.66% and 0.64%, respectively. These gains exceed the heuristic overlap assignment gains (0.15% DER, 0.08% JER).

The proposed post-processing method iteratively selects two speakers from clustering-based diarization results, extracts frames containing only those two, and refines them with a two-speaker EEND model. Evaluations on CALLHOME, AMI, and DIHARD II datasets show consistent improvement over various clustering-based diarization baselines.
